items.foco: Spinal cord injury at C1-C4 level causes diaphragmatic paralysis
items.title: Upper cervical spinal cord injury and diaphragmatic paralysis
items.contentHtml:
C1-C4 spinal cord injury

  • The phrenic nerve (C3-C5 roots) is interrupted in complete lesions above C4, producing diaphragmatic paralysis and immediate ventilatory dependence.
  • The lesion at the C3-C4 level also affects the function of the ipsilateral diaphragm; Below C5, spontaneous breathing is preserved.
  • In trauma, the diagnostic triad includes: hypoventilation, elevation of the diaphragm on chest x-ray, and paradoxical movement on fluoroscopy.
  • Acute management requires orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation; phrenic nerve stimulation is an option in stable chronic lesions.

items.foco: Axillary nerve injury affects arm abduction
items.title: Axillary nerve injury and abduction
items.contentHtml:
Axillary nerve

  • The axillary nerve (C5-C6) innervates the deltoid (abduction of the arm from 15° to 90°) and the teres minor (external rotation).
  • Its most common injury is due to fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus or anterior glenohumeral dislocation.
  • Clinically: disability to abduct the arm beyond the first 15° (supraspinatus onset of abduction) and loss of sensation in the deltoid region (epaulette patch).
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with electromyography; Conservative treatment includes physiotherapy and, if there is no recovery in 3-6 months, surgical neurotization.

items.foco: Injury to the radial nerve produces hanging wrist or drop
items.title: Radial nerve injury and hanging wrist
items.contentHtml:
Radial nerve

  • The radial nerve (C5-C8) innervates the extensors of the forearm, hand and fingers. Its high injury (due to a humerus fracture in the torsion canal) produces a wrist drop. There is an inability to extend the wrist and fingers at the metacarpophalangeal level, with preservation of digital flexion and interphalangeal extension (innervated by the ulnar). thumb).
  • The differential diagnosis includes injury to the posterior interosseous nerve (without sensory deficit). Treatment is conservative with a splint; If there is no improvement after 4 months, neurotization or tendon transfer is indicated.

items.foco: Median nerve injury causes preacher's hand
items.title: Median nerve injury and preacher's hand
items.contentHtml:
Median nerve

  • The median nerve (C6-T1) innervates most of the forearm flexors and the thenar eminence. The complete proximal lesion produces preacher's hand (blessing sign): inability to flex the index and middle fingers when making a fist.
  • It is associated with loss of thumb opposition and thenar atrophy.
  • The lesion at the level of the carpus (carpal tunnel syndrome) causes paresthesias in the first 3 fingers and night pain, with Tinel's sign and Phalen positive.
  • Treatment of acute traumatic injury is microsurgical repair; In the carpal tunnel, the first line is a night splint and NSAIDs, and if that fails, corticosteroid infiltration or surgical release.

items.foco: Ulnar nerve injury causes ulnar claw
items.title: Ulnar nerve and ulnar claw injury
items.contentHtml:
Ulnar nerve

  • The ulnar nerve (C8-T1) innervates the intrinsic muscles of the hand (interossei, 3rd and 4th lumbricals, hypothenar) and the flexor carpi ulnaris.
  • Complete injury produces ulnar claw: metacarpophalangeal hyperextension and interphalangeal flexion of the fingers 4° and 5°, with atrophy of the first interosseous space and hypothenar eminence.
  • The Froment sign is key: flexion of the thumb when trying to hold a paper (compensation for flexor pollicis longus, innervated by the median).
  • The most common causes are fracture of the medial epicondyle, elbow dislocation or compression in Guyon's canal. Ulnar neuropathy in the elbow is treated with surgical decompression if it does not respond to conservative measures.

items.foco: Injury to the common peroneal nerve causes foot drop
items.title: Common peroneal nerve injury and foot drop
items.contentHtml:
Common peroneal nerve

  • The common peroneal nerve (L4-S2) divides into the deep and superficial peroneal nerves; Its injury produces drop foot due to paralysis of the dorsiflexors and eversors of the foot. Clinically: inability to dorsiflex the ankle and fingers, with steppage gait and sensory loss on the dorsum of the foot and anterolateral aspect of the leg. The most frequent causes are compression in the neck of the fibula. (prolonged sitting posture, casts, fractures) or iatrogenic injury.
  • Differential diagnosis includes L5 radiculopathy and sciatic neuropathy. Initial treatment is ankle-foot orthoses; If there is no recovery in 3 months, surgical decompression is considered.

items.foco: Pituitary stalk injury disrupts ADH and diabetes insipidus
items.title: Pituitary stalk injury and diabetes insipidus
items.contentHtml:
Pituitary stalk

  • The pituitary stalk (infundibulum) connects the hypothalamus with the neurohypophysis; its section interrupts the transport of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin, causing central diabetes insipidus.
  • Clinically: polyuria (>40 ml/kg/day), polydipsia, hypotonic urine (density <1.005, osmolality <300 mOsm/kg) and hypernatremia if there is no access to the water.
  • The water deprivation test confirms the diagnosis: urinary osmolality does not concentrate >300 mOsm/kg after 4-18 hours, but increases after administering desmopressin.
  • The treatment of choice is desmopressin (ADH analog) intranasal, oral or subcutaneous, adjusting the dose to maintain normonatremia.

items.foco: Frontal lobe lesion produces Broca's aphasia
items.title: Frontal lobe injury and Broca's aphasia
items.contentHtml:
Frontal lobe

  • Broca's area (frontal operculum, Brodmann area 44-45) in the dominant hemisphere controls language production. Its lesion generates Broca's aphasia (motor, non-fluent). (grasping).
  • The most common cause is ischemic stroke in the middle cerebral artery (upper branch). Recovery depends on neuronal plasticity; Speech therapy is essential.

items.foco: Temporal lobe injury causes Wernicke's aphasia
items.title: Temporal lobe lesion and Wernicke's aphasia
items.contentHtml:
Temporal lobe

  • Wernicke's area (posterior superior temporal gyrus, Brodmann area 22) in the dominant hemisphere processes language comprehension. His lesion produces Wernicke's aphasia (sensitive, fluent).
  • The patient speaks fluently, but with paraphasias (word substitutions) and neologisms; comprehension is severely affected, and repetition is deficient.
  • It is usually accompanied by contralateral superior homonymous hemianopsia due to involvement of the optic radiation in Meyer's loop.
  • The most common cause is ischemic stroke in the middle cerebral artery (inferior branch). Treatment includes speech therapy; the prognosis for understanding is guarded.

items.foco: Cerebellar lesion causes ataxia and dysmetria
items.title: Cerebellar injury and ataxia
items.contentHtml:
Cerebellum

  • Lesions of the cerebellum (hemispheric or vermian) produce ataxia (incoordination of movement), dysmetria (failure in the distance of movement) and dysdiadochokinesia (difficulty with rapid alternating movements).
  • The Romberg sign is negative in ataxia. cerebellar (worse with eyes open), unlike sensory ataxia.
  • Vermis lesion causes axial ataxia (trunk and gait), while hemispheric lesion produces ipsilateral appendiceal ataxia (limbs).
  • Etiologies include stroke (cerebellar arteries), tumors (medulloblastoma in children), multiple sclerosis, or alcohol intoxication (cerebellar degeneration).

items.foco: Injury to the basal ganglia produces hemiballismus or chorea
items.title: Basal ganglia injury and hemiballismus
items.contentHtml:
Basal ganglia

  • The basal ganglia regulate voluntary movement through direct and indirect pathways. Injury to the subthalamic nucleus (body of Luys) produces hemibalism: sudden, large, and high-amplitude movements in one hemibody.
  • chorea (rapid, irregular, non-repetitive movements) is associated with dysfunction of the striatum (caudate and putamen), typical in Huntington's disease (CAG triplet expansion in huntingtin gene).
  • Treatment of acute hemiballismus includes dopamine receptor blockers (haloperidol) or tetrabenazine; in Huntington's chorea, the first line is tetrabenazine or atypical antipsychotics.
  • The differential diagnosis includes ischemic lesions of the subthalamic nucleus (perforating branch of the posterior cerebral artery).

items.foco: Facial nerve (Bell) injury causes peripheral facial paralysis
items.title: Bell's palsy and facial nerve
items.contentHtml:
Facial nerve

  • Bell's palsy is an acute idiopathic peripheral facial palsy of the facial nerve (VII nerve). It affects the entire face: forehead, eye and mouth, with the inability to close the eye (lagopthalmos).
  • The diagnosis is clinical; herpes zoster oticus (Ramsay Hunt syndrome) should be ruled out if there are vesicles in the external ear canal or hearing loss.
  • Treatment with prednisone 60 mg/day for 7 days (started within 72 hours) improves recovery. Routine acyclovir is not recommended, except in Ramsay Hunt.
  • Most recover completely in 3-6 months; electromyography at 2 weeks predicts the evolution (denervation >90% indicates poor prognosis).
